body_txt: When a work has a large and active fanbase, but seems to be virtually unknown by mainstream audiences, who are often surprised the work in question has a fanbase at all, much less such a large one. Essentially an inverse of Mainstream Obscurity . Where a work suffering from Mainstream Obscurity has had a huge influence on popular culture and is often referenced by all kinds of people, but has a comparatively small fan base who have actually seen/read/played the work in question, works suffering from Obscure Popularity are almost never referenced in the mainstream, and don't seem to ring a bell for a lot of people not actively involved in the fandom, but said fandom actually has a lot of members. Usually, it comes down to the types of audiences the work in question is aimed at, since a work may have a large audience but it won't contribute to pop culture if said audience doesn't contribute to pop culture. There are many types of media that are prone to this, but some more than others. For New Media , this situation is pretty much a given to an extent. It is very difficult for online fandoms to break into offline culture, so even if a work manages to be popular and influential on the internet, with frequent references by other New Media and online discussions, you'll more often than not still be hard-pressed to find even a passing reference from traditional media. Even the most famous Internet personalities with millions of followers/subscribers are still separate from mainstream celebrities. Since Internet personalities are generally more open, they can usually just update fans themselves, rather than needing mainstream outlets to write about them or interview them—and this lack of coverage means mainstream audiences don't learn about them. (Note that there are also other major factors to the lack of discussion of the internet by mainstream outlets.) Conversely, works aimed at audiences who are less online can be underrepresented in online discourse relative to their commercial popularity. In particular, works targeted towards older audiences (such as procedurals , soap operas , and video games realistically simulating real-world jobs and sports ) often lack the attributes that drive the small but highly passionate and productive (and usually younger) crowd to produce online discussions, fanfiction , Fan Art , Cosplay and similar types of derivative contents that make an online fandom more noticeable. These works are more likely to be mentioned by traditional media outlets than the majority of popular New Media , although they're still liable to only discuss surface level attributes of the work depending on what it is. Any media aimed at kids (very young kids in particular) will commonly fall to this as well; the audience is massive, but also barely capable of discussing the work (at least until they grow up, but by that point they have most likely moved on to media aimed at slightly older children; these later kid shows aren't as easily forgotten). Casual Video Games as well; they are played by millions of people worldwide and are amongst the most popular series out there, but by their very nature they're only meant to be consumed casually ( like the name of the genre suggests ) and thus don't tend to produce further discussion online or even in mainstream outlets. note For reference, an example of a casual game that avoided this fate would be Wordle . Informational works such as wikis are also possible contenders, but they can teeter the line between "audience" and "fanbase" in the first place. Can overlap with First Installment Wins , when mainstream audiences know about the first work in a series or franchise, but are unaware that there are other works in it. Related to Americans Hate Tingle and Pop-Culture Isolation .
